Bangalore Apartments’ Federation (BAF)

This information pertains to AOAs who have requests for individual charging stations from their residents and how to handle the same!

🚗 Section 01
EV Vehicle Anatomy

Click any component card to learn more about each part of an electric vehicle.

⚙

Electric Motor

Converts electrical energy into mechanical power to drive the wheels.

Tap to learn more ›
The electric motor receives power from the battery via the motor drive and converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy. Most modern EVs use Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) for high efficiency and instant torque delivery.
🔄

Transmission

Transfers torque from the electric motor to the drive wheels.

Tap to learn more ›
Unlike ICE vehicles, EVs typically use a single-speed fixed-ratio transmission. This simplifies the drivetrain significantly and enables smooth, instant acceleration without the need for gear changes.
🎗

Motor Drive

Controls and regulates power delivery to the electric motor.

Tap to learn more ›
The motor drive (inverter/controller) converts DC power from the battery into AC power for the motor. It controls motor speed and torque based on driver input and enables regenerative braking during deceleration.
🔄

Battery Converter

Converts high-voltage battery power for 12V auxiliary systems.

Tap to learn more ›
The DC-DC battery converter steps down the high-voltage traction battery (300–800V) to 12V for powering auxiliary systems like lights, infotainment, and control electronics — similar to an alternator in a conventional vehicle.
📦

On-board Charger

Converts incoming AC from the grid to DC voltage for battery charging.

Tap to learn more ›
The on-board charger (OBC) sits inside the vehicle and rectifies AC power from the charging station into the correct DC voltage needed to charge the traction battery. Power ratings range from 3.3 kW to 22 kW.
🔋

Traction Battery Bank

The main energy store — large lithium-ion pack powering the vehicle.

Tap to learn more ›
The traction battery bank is the primary energy source of the EV. Typically lithium-ion cells arranged in modules, housed under the vehicle floor. Capacity ranges from 20 kWh to 100+ kWh, managed by a Battery Management System (BMS).
🔋

Auxiliary Battery

Powers vehicle accessories and control systems at 12V.

Tap to learn more ›
The auxiliary battery provides 12V power for accessories, control modules, and safety systems when the main traction battery is off. It is recharged by the DC-DC converter while driving.
🔌

Charger Port

The inlet on the vehicle body where the charging cable connects.

Tap to learn more ›
The charger port is the physical connection point on the vehicle for accepting power from an external charging station. It supports AC (Type-2) and/or DC (CCS-2, CHAdeMO) connectors depending on the vehicle standard.
🔋 Section 02
Components of EV

Click each component to expand full details.

🔌

Charge Inlet

Point where the AC connector are plugged into the vehicle

Tap to expand ›
The Charge Inlet is the entry point on the vehicle where the AC charging connector is physically plugged in. It acts as the gateway for electrical energy transfer from the external charging equipment into the vehicle’s onboard charging system.
AC ChargingCCSType-2
🔋

Battery Bank

Another major component of EV

Tap to expand ›
The Battery Bank is another major component of an EV. It stores the electrical energy needed to power the vehicle. Typically made of lithium-ion cells arranged in modules, it determines the vehicle’s range, performance, and weight.
Li-IonBMS40–100+ kWh
🏠

Charge Unit

Point where the EB connection is terminated

Tap to expand ›
The Charge Unit is the point where the Electricity Board (EB) connection is terminated for EV charging. It is the physical charging station or wallbox that safely delivers metered electrical power from the grid to the vehicle.
EVSEWallboxLevel 2
💻

Power Control Unit

Plays important role in EV safety and management

Tap to expand ›
The Power Control Unit plays an important role in EV safety and management. It integrates the inverter, motor controller, and DC-DC converter, managing power flow between the battery, motor, and auxiliary systems.
InverterSafetyRegen Braking
⚙

Electric Motor

Major Component for the electric vehicle

Tap to expand ›
The Electric Motor is a Major Component for the electric vehicle. It converts electrical energy from the battery into mechanical energy to propel the vehicle. Most EVs use PMSM motors delivering near-instant torque at 85–95% efficiency.
PMSMHigh Torque85–95% eff.
📦

On Board Charger

Converts incoming AC from the grid to appropriate DC voltage for battery charging.

Tap to expand ›
The On Board Charger converts incoming AC from the grid to appropriate DC voltage for battery charging. It is mounted inside the vehicle and manages the conversion process. Ratings range from 3.3 kW to 22 kW.
AC to DC3.3–22 kWPFC

⚡ Section 03
Types of EV Chargers

A complete comparison of all EV charger types — standards, power ratings, use cases and vehicle categories.

Charger TypeStandard / ConnectorPower RatingCharging LevelTypical Use CaseVehicle Category
AC Slow ChargerBharat AC0013.3 kW × 3 outputsLevel 1 / SlowPublic/semi-public slow charging, offices, apartments2W, 3W, small 4W
AC Fast ChargerType-2 (IEC 62196)7.4 kW / 11 kW / 22 kWLevel 2Homes, commercial buildings, malls4W, e-cars
DC Slow ChargerBharat DC00115 kWDC Fast (Entry)Fleets, e-autos, e-rickshaws3W, small 4W
DC Fast ChargerCCS-230 kW – 60 kWFastHighways, public stations, premium EVs4W cars, SUVs
DC Fast ChargerCHAdeMO50 kWFastLegacy Japanese EVsLimited 4W
DC Ultra-Fast ChargerCCS-2 (HPC)100 kW – 350 kWUltra-FastHighways, intercity corridorsPremium EVs, buses (adapted)
GB/T ChargerGB/T AC & DC30–200 kWFastElectric buses (older FAME-I)E-buses
Pantograph ChargerOppCharge / CCS150–600 kWUltra-FastBus depots, BRTS, metro feeder busesE-buses
Battery Swapping StationProprietaryN/AInstantFleets, 2W/3W commercial2W, 3W
Home ChargerType-2 / Wallbox3.3–7.4 kWLevel 1/2Residential parking4W
Portable ChargerType-2 / 15A Plug1.5–3.3 kWSlowEmergency, home use4W
⚠ Section 04
Challenges of EV in RWA

10 key challenges Resident Welfare Associations face when enabling EV charging for residents.

01

⚡ Limited electrical load

The building doesn’t have enough spare power to support many chargers.

02

💰 High setup cost

Wiring, meters, panels, and safety devices are expensive.

03

🔥 Fire safety worries

RWAs fear battery fires and lack proper safety systems.

04

📜 Confusing rules

Many RWAs are unsure about government, DISCOM, and safety norms.

05

🧾 Billing difficulties

Hard to track each resident’s charging usage and collect payments.

06

🅿 Parking space issues

Not all parking spots can easily get cables or power lines.

07

🥇 Unreliable vendors

Some vendors give incomplete, unsafe, or misleading solutions.

08

⚠ Risk of overload

Too many EVs charging at once can trip the building’s supply.

09

🏠 Resident disagreements

Different expectations: slow vs fast chargers, cost sharing, safety concerns.

10

📅 No long-term plan

RWAs don’t know how many EVs will come in future, making planning difficult.

🧠 Challenge Quiz

How well do you know the EV challenges in RWA?

🔥 Section 05
Critical Root cause for EV Chargers Catching fire

10 critical root causes and their effects — every RWA must be aware of these fire risks.

CAUSE 01

🏷 Poor-quality or uncertified chargers

Low-grade EVSE without IS/IEC certification can overheat, fail under load, or lack proper protection.

CAUSE 02

🔧 Improper wiring or loose connections

Loose terminals, undersized cables, or poor crimping increase resistance and heat buildup.

CAUSE 03

🔌 Overloading of circuits

Multiple chargers on the same circuit can exceed capacity, causing overheating of cables and breakers.

CAUSE 04

🛡 Absence of RCCB/RCBO (Type-A/Type-B)

Without proper leakage protection, DC leakage or faults may go undetected, stressing the system.

CAUSE 05

⚡ No surge protection

Voltage surges from grid fluctuations or lightning can damage chargers and cause internal failures.

CAUSE 06

🌎 Poor earthing or high earth resistance

Inadequate earthing leads to unsafe fault currents and overheating of metallic parts.

CAUSE 07

🔋 Use of extension cords or makeshift wiring

Temporary cables are not designed for continuous high-power charging and can overheat.

CAUSE 08

💨 Blocked ventilation around charger

Chargers need airflow; heat accumulation accelerates component failure.

CAUSE 09

🧯 Charging in areas with flammable materials

Storing cardboard, oils, or waste near chargers increases fire risk if overheating occurs.

CAUSE 10

🔩 Lack of periodic maintenance

Dust, moisture, loose screws, and aging components increase the chance of electrical faults.

🛡 Section 06
EV Charging – Risk-Mitigation Plan for RWA

A 15-point structured plan — what every RWA should do to ensure safe EV charging infrastructure.

Risk AreaRoot RiskMitigation Plan (What RWA Should Do)Outcome / Benefit
1. Electrical LoadBuilding may not have enough powerConduct load study; get DISCOM approval; plan phased load upgradesPrevents overloads and tripping
2. Wiring SafetyPoor-quality or old wiringUse FRLS/FRLSH cables; proper conduit; certified electricianReduces overheating and fire risk
3. Overloading CircuitsToo many chargers on one lineUse dedicated circuits; load balancing; staggered chargingEnsures stable and safe power flow
4. Faulty ChargersCheap or uncertified chargersAllow only BIS-certified, DISCOM-approved chargersPrevents equipment failure and sparks
5. No Safety DevicesMissing MCB/RCCB/RCBOMandatory installation of protection devices for each chargerCuts off power during faults
6. Damaged CablesCrushed or exposed wiresRegular inspection; cable guards; proper routingAvoids short circuits and shocks
7. Water ExposureRainwater entering socketsUse IP65/IP67 outdoor-rated chargers; weatherproof boxesPrevents moisture-related fires
8. Poor VentilationHeat buildup in parkingEnsure airflow; avoid enclosed corners; add exhaust fans if neededKeeps chargers and batteries cool
9. Battery Issues in EVsWeak or damaged batteriesEncourage OEM service checks; no charging of damaged EVsReduces thermal runaway risk
10. Untrained InstallationUnsafe wiring by local electriciansUse licensed electricians; follow IS/IEC standardsEnsures safe, compliant installation
11. Wrong Charger TypeMismatch between EV and chargerApprove only OEM-compatible chargersPrevents overheating and battery stress
12. Parking Layout IssuesCables blocking pathwaysUse cable trays; overhead routing; floor guardsPrevents accidents and cable damage
13. Lack of MonitoringNo tracking of faults or heatInstall smart chargers with alerts; periodic checksEarly detection of problems
14. No Fire Safety MeasuresNo extinguishers or alarmsInstall CO₂ extinguishers; smoke detectors; emergency cut-offEnables quick response to incidents
15. No RWA PolicyResidents install chargers randomlyCreate EV Charging Policy with rules, approvals, safety normsEnsures uniform, safe implementation

🏆 Final Knowledge Quiz

Test your complete understanding of EV charging safety for RWAs

✅ Check List for AOAs
Individual EV Charging Approval Checklist for AOAs

Before Approving Any Individual EV Charging Request — go through all 10 sections.

📋 SECTION 1: Governance & Policy Readiness
›
✔️ Policy Framework
  • Is there a written EV Charging Policy?
  • Has it been approved by MC/EC / General Body (if required)?
  • Does it clearly define: Eligibility criteria, Approved charger types, Installation process, Safety requirements, Cost responsibility, Billing mechanism
❗ Pain Point: Residents install chargers randomly without approval.
✓ How to Handle:
  • Make prior approval mandatory
  • Communicate policy clearly to all residents
  • Disallow unauthorized installations
⚡ SECTION 2: Electrical Infrastructure Readiness CRITICAL CORE
›
⚡ 2.1 Transformer Capacity & Load Assessment
  • Total transformer capacity (kVA)
  • Current peak load vs average load
  • Available spare capacity
  • EV load impact (simultaneous charging scenario)
  • Diversity factor consideration
❗ Pain Point: "Will transformer fail if multiple EVs charge together?"
⚠ Risk:
  • Overloading
  • Tripping
  • Reduced transformer life
  • High upgrade costs
✓ Action:
  • Conduct professional load study
  • Approve limited number of EVs
  • Implement phased approvals
  • Prefer common charging model
🏠 2.2 Individual Flat Load & Sanctioned Capacity
  • Flat’s sanctioned load
  • Current usage vs available capacity
  • Requirement for load enhancement
❗ Pain Point: Residents try to use power beyond sanctioned limits.
⚠ Risk:
  • Meter overloading
  • Unauthorized usage
  • Billing disputes
✓ Action:
  • EV charging only within sanctioned load OR after formal enhancement
  • No usage from common supply
🧱 2.3 Common Panel Boards & Distribution Risk VERY CRITICAL
  • Panel board condition
  • Busbar capacity
  • Feeder availability
  • Space for additional breakers
  • Segregation between flats
❗ Pain Point: "If one charger fails, will it affect other flats?"
⚠ Risk:
  • Short circuit impacting multiple flats
  • Panel damage
  • Fire propagation
✓ Action:
  • Provide dedicated EV feeder lines
  • Ensure proper isolation
  • Upgrade panels if required
🔌 2.4 Wiring Infrastructure & Routing
  • Cable length and routing feasibility
  • Cable sizing adequacy
  • Exposure to water / damage
  • Safe routing (no obstruction)
❗ Pain Point: Basement wiring becomes messy and unsafe.
⚠ Risk:
  • Cable damage
  • Short circuits
  • Safety hazards
✓ Action:
  • Use FRLS/FRLSH cables and Conduits / cable trays
  • Avoid loose wiring and extension cords
⚡ 2.5 Protection & Isolation Systems
  • Dedicated MCB
  • RCCB / RCBO installed
  • Surge protection (if required)
  • Proper earthing
❗ Pain Point: "How do we isolate faults quickly?"
⚠ Risk:
  • Fault spreading
  • Fire escalation
  • Electric shock
✓ Action:
  • Mandatory independent protection for each charger
  • No shared circuits
⚖ 2.6 Liability, Indemnity & Responsibility
  • Indemnity bond from resident
  • Installation compliance certificate
  • Defined liability boundaries
❗ Pain Point: "If something goes wrong, who is responsible?"
⚠ Risk:
  • Legal disputes
  • Financial liability on AOA
✓ Action:
  • Mandatory indemnity
  • Align with insurance
  • Document all approvals
🔄 2.7 System-Level Risk Understanding
⚠ Reality: EV charging affects entire electrical ecosystem
  • Transformer overload
  • Panel failure
  • Multi-flat outages
  • Fire risk
  • Legal exposure
🚫 2.8 Strategic Insight
❗ Key Message: Individual EV charging creates fragmented and risky infrastructure.
✓ Recommended Direction: Prefer Common Charging Infrastructure
  • Central control
  • Better safety
  • Scalable
  • Lower long-term cost
🔧 SECTION 3: Technical Feasibility (Per Request)
›
  • Parking slot identified
  • Cable routing feasible
  • No obstruction to: Walkways, Fire exits, Vehicle movement
❗ Pain Point: Unsafe cable routing in parking areas.
✓ Action:
  • Structured routing
  • Avoid floor-level cables
🔌 SECTION 4: Charger & Equipment Compliance
›
  • BIS / IS / IEC certified charger
  • OEM compatibility
  • AC home charger type
❗ Pain Point: Vendor confusion and overselling.
✓ Action:
  • Approve only standard chargers
  • Avoid DC fast chargers in basements
🛡 SECTION 5: Electrical Safety Requirements
›
  • Dedicated circuit
  • Protection devices installed
  • Earthing verified
❗ Pain Point: Fear of fire incidents.
✓ Action:
  • Strict compliance enforcement
🔧 SECTION 6: Installation Standards
›
  • Licensed electrician
  • Proper cable sizing
  • No temporary wiring
❗ Pain Point: Poor installation quality.
✓ Action:
  • Allow only qualified installers
  • Post-install inspection
🔥 SECTION 7: Fire Safety Preparedness
›
  • No flammable materials nearby
  • Adequate ventilation
  • Fire extinguishers available
  • Emergency cutoff available
❗ Pain Point: Basement fire risk.
✓ Action:
  • Define charging zones
  • Maintain clean environment
🧮 SECTION 8: Metering & Billing
›
  • Individual metering
  • Usage tracking system
  • Defined billing model
❗ Pain Point: Billing disputes.
✓ Action:
  • Meter-based billing only
  • Transparent system
🧭 SECTION 9: Monitoring & Maintenance
›
  • Inspection schedule
  • Fault monitoring system
  • Maintenance logs
❗ Pain Point: No monitoring after approval.
✓ Action:
  • Annual audits
  • Regular inspections
⚖ SECTION 10: Documentation & Compliance
›
  • Application recorded
  • Approval documented
  • Installation drawings available
  • Test certificates maintained
❗ Pain Point: No documentation → no control.
✓ Action:
  • Maintain central EV registry
  • Ensure traceability
🍎 FINAL APPROVAL DECISION MATRIX

❌ DO NOT APPROVE if ANY of the below:

  • No policy
  • No load study
  • No panel validation
  • No safety systems
  • No indemnity

✅ APPROVE ONLY if ALL are satisfied:

  • Policy exists
  • Electrical capacity validated
  • Certified equipment used
  • Safe installation completed
  • Billing system defined
  • Documentation complete

💡 FINAL STRATEGIC MESSAGE

"Individual EV charging may look simple…
but its risks are always shared by the entire community."

🚀 "Don’t approve EV charging requests individually.
Approve them through a structured system."
⚖ Section 08
Karnataka High Court Judgment

Naveen Nagendra vs Sunil Desai & Others — WA No. 979 of 2024 — Dated 3rd February, 2026

Karnataka High Court — Official Judgment
Naveen Nagendra vs Sunil Desai & Others — WA No. 979 of 2024 — Dated 3rd February, 2026
NC: 2026:KHC:6034-DB — Reproduced as-is. No alterations.
⬇ Download PDF

Your browser does not support inline PDF viewing.

⬇ Download the Judgment PDF
Case Citation
NC: 2026:KHC:6034-DB
Case Number
WA No. 979 of 2024 C/W CCC No. 495 of 2024
Court
High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
Date of Judgment
3rd February, 2026
Coram
Hon’ble Mr. Vibhu Bakhru, Chief Justice & Hon’ble Mr. Justice C.M. Poonacha
Appellant
Shriram Suhaana Apartments Owners Association (Rep. by President Sunil Desai)
Respondent No. 1
Mr. Naveen Nagendra, Flat Owner, Shriram Suhaana
Outcome
Disposed of by Consensus — Petitioner Permitted to Install EVCS
📄 Background & Nature of Proceedings
›

This matter arose from a Writ Petition (W.P. No. 4253/2023) filed by Mr. Naveen Nagendra, a flat owner in Shriram Suhaana Apartment Complex, Bengaluru, seeking directions to the Apartment Owners Association and BESCOM to consider his request for an individual EV charging point in his allotted parking slot.

The learned Single Judge disposed of the Writ Petition directing the Association to consult experts and take a decision within four weeks. The Association challenged this order by filing Writ Appeal No. 979 of 2024 before the Division Bench. Simultaneously, the flat owner filed CCC No. 495/2024 alleging wilful disobedience of the Single Judge’s order.

📅 Both the Writ Appeal and the Contempt Petition were taken up together and disposed of by the Division Bench on 3rd February, 2026 by consensus.
⚖ Legal Issues Raised
›
  • Whether a flat owner has the right to install an individual EV Charging Station (EVCS) in a multi-storeyed apartment complex.
  • Whether the Apartment Owners Association can refuse or delay such a request.
  • Whether the Association’s concerns about unsafe crisscross wiring (due to parking slots being in different towers) constituted a valid ground to deny installation.
  • Maintainability of the original Writ Petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India.
  • Whether the Association was in wilful disobedience of the Single Judge’s order (Contempt).
⚠ The Division Bench did not examine the maintainability and other legal questions in view of the consensus reached between the parties.
📚 Applicable Laws & Guidelines
›
  • Rule 15, Electricity (Rights of Consumers) Amendment Rules, 2024 — Entitles flat owners with allotted parking to approach BESCOM directly for an EV charging connection.
  • BESCOM Operational Guidelines for EVCS — Permits individual flat owners allocated a specific parking space in multi-storeyed buildings to install EV charging stations within their existing sanctioned load or by load enhancement.
  • CEA Safety Standards/Regulations — Governs the appropriate wiring/cabling from the metering panel to the charging point.
  • Article 215, Constitution of India r/w Sections 11 & 12, Contempt of Courts Act, 1971 — Basis for the Civil Contempt Petition.
  • Section 4, Karnataka High Court Act — Basis for filing the Writ Appeal before the Division Bench.

📄 Key BESCOM Guideline (As Quoted by the Court)

“Individual Flat owners/consumers/occupiers who are allocated a specific parking space in Multi-storeyed buildings/Complexes and wishing to install EV charging stations/points for all type of vehicles including two wheelers, are allowed to connect such units within their existing sanctioned load or, if necessary, enhance the load as per the requirement with the approval of the Distribution licensee under the existing Installation category tariff… The appropriate wiring/cabling shall be extended from the Metering Panel to where the charging stations/points are located complying with the CEA safety standards/Regulations.”

✅ Final Order & Directions of the Division Bench
›

The Division Bench, in view of the consensus between all parties, passed the following directions:

  • Since the petitioner’s car parking slot is in the same tower as his apartment, he is at liberty to install EVCS in accordance with BESCOM guidelines.
  • The petitioner shall ensure the installation does not cause any inconvenience or risk to any other apartment owner.
  • The Writ Appeal (WA No. 979/2024) stands disposed of.
  • The Civil Contempt Petition (CCC No. 495/2024) stands closed.
  • The pending interlocutory application also stands disposed of.
✅ Verdict: The flat owner’s right to install an individual EVCS in his designated parking slot was upheld. The Association’s appeal was disposed of without any relief to the Association.
📚 Key Legal Takeaways for AOAs & RWAs
›
  • A flat owner has a legal right to install an individual EV Charging Station in his allotted parking slot under BESCOM Operational Guidelines and Rule 15 of the Electricity (Rights of Consumers) Amendment Rules, 2024.
  • AOAs cannot arbitrarily refuse such requests. Refusal without expert consultation or valid technical grounds may expose the Association’s office bearers to contempt proceedings.
  • Expert consultation is mandatory before arriving at any decision on EVCS requests — this was the direction of the Single Judge that was not challenged by the Division Bench.
  • Wiring concerns (crisscross wiring due to parking in different towers) are a valid consideration but not an automatic ground for refusal — they must be examined by a qualified expert.
  • Installation must comply with BESCOM guidelines and CEA safety standards. The flat owner bears responsibility to ensure no inconvenience or risk to other residents.
  • The Association’s liability is limited when a proper structured approval process with indemnity and expert assessment is followed — which is precisely what the BAF Checklist mandates.
💡 BAF Guidance: This judgment reinforces why AOAs must have a written EV Charging Policy and a structured approval process in place BEFORE residents make requests. Ad-hoc decisions invite legal challenges. Follow the BAF Checklist to protect your Association.
